A-003-001-001: On an oscilloscope you see the full vertical extent of the trace directly, so the easiest reading is from the bottom of the wave to the top, which is the peak-to-peak voltage. Peak and RMS require you to find the centre line or do a 0.707 conversion, and average is not shown at all, so each of those takes an extra step.
A-003-001-002: For a pure sine wave, RMS = peak × 0.707, and peak = half the peak-to-peak. Here peak = 340 ÷ 2 = 170 V, so RMS = 170 × 0.707 ≈ 120 V. The tempting distractor 170 V is the peak value, not the RMS value.
A-003-001-003: RMS (root-mean-square) is defined by heating effect: it is the AC voltage that delivers the same power to a resistor as a DC voltage of equal value. The wrong answers either compare AC to the peak (not RMS) or invent a 'square root of peak' rule, but RMS comes from averaging the square of the waveform, not from a simple square root.
A-003-001-004: For a sine wave RMS = peak × 0.707. With a peak of 20 V, RMS = 20 × 0.707 ≈ 14.14 V. The frequency (100 Hz) is irrelevant to the conversion; 28.28 V would be peak-to-peak and 7.07 V comes from dividing instead of multiplying.
A-003-001-005: Ohm's law for AC normally uses RMS values, and RMS = peak × 0.707, so 'peak values times 0.707' is correct. Average values do not give true power and would mislead Ohm's-law calculations, which is why the average-based options are wrong.
A-003-001-006: The effective (RMS) value of a sine wave is peak × 0.707, i.e. 70.7% of the maximum. The distractor 63.6% is the average value of a rectified sine (2/π), a common trap, so do not confuse average with effective.
A-003-001-007: AC voltmeters are calibrated in RMS because RMS is the value used for power and Ohm's-law work and matches how AC mains is rated. They do not display peak, instantaneous, or average values, even though the internal sensor may actually respond to average and be scaled to read RMS.
A-003-001-008: Effective value and RMS mean the same thing, and AC voltmeters are scaled to read it because RMS is what determines heating and power. Peak, peak-to-peak, and average are larger or smaller scalings of the same waveform and are not what the meter is set up to show.
A-003-001-009: By definition, the RMS value of an AC voltage produces the same heating in a resistor as an equal DC voltage. Peak and peak-to-peak are larger numbers that overstate the heating, and average understates it, so only RMS gives the true equivalent power.
A-003-001-010: Work outward from RMS: peak = RMS × 1.414, then peak-to-peak = 2 × peak. So peak = 120 × 1.414 ≈ 169.7 V, and peak-to-peak = 2 × 169.7 ≈ 339.5 V. The distractor 169.7 V is only the peak, missing the final doubling.
A-003-001-011: For a sine wave RMS = peak × 0.707. With a 17 V peak, RMS = 17 × 0.707 ≈ 12 V. The value 34 V would be peak-to-peak (twice the peak), not the RMS.
A-003-002-001: Peak-envelope power (PEP) is the average power over one RF cycle at the highest crest of the modulation envelope. It is the standard way amateur output power is specified for SSB; carrier power and mean power are lower figures that do not capture the modulation peak.
A-003-002-002: Multiplying peak-envelope voltage by 0.707 gives the RMS voltage, and RMS² ÷ R is power, so this procedure yields PEP (peak-envelope power). PIV is a diode voltage rating and ERP is radiated power, so neither comes from this voltage-and-resistance calculation.
A-003-002-003: PEP is found from voltage and resistance: take peak-envelope voltage × 0.707 to get RMS, square it, and divide by the load resistance (P = V²RMS / R). It is a real, measurable quantity, not hypothetical, and 'peak voltage × peak current' overstates the true average power at the crest.
A-003-002-004: With only a voltmeter you measure voltage across a known resistive load, so the power formula is P = E² / R. The forms P = EI/R and P = IR are dimensionally wrong for power, and P = EI cos θ needs current and a phase angle you cannot get from a voltmeter alone.
A-003-002-005: Using a scope you read peak-envelope voltage (PEV), convert to RMS with 0.707, then apply P = V²RMS / RL, giving PEP = [(0.707 PEV)(0.707 PEV)] / RL. The options using Vp directly or 1.414 omit the RMS conversion and would give the wrong power.
A-003-002-006: First convert: peak = 200 ÷ 2 = 100 V, then RMS = 100 × 0.707 ≈ 70.7 V. PEP = V²RMS / R = 70.7² / 50 ≈ 5000 / 50 = 100 W. Forgetting to halve the peak-to-peak or to apply 0.707 is what produces the larger wrong answers.
A-003-002-007: Peak = 500 ÷ 2 = 250 V, RMS = 250 × 0.707 ≈ 176.8 V, and PEP = V²RMS / R = 176.8² / 50 ≈ 31250 / 50 = 625 W. A shortcut is PEP = (Vpp)² / (8R) = 500² / 400 = 625 W, which folds the halving and 0.707 into one step.
A-003-002-008: For an unmodulated carrier the signal is a steady sine wave, so its envelope never rises above the average level and PEP equals the average power. The wattmeter reads 1060 W average, so PEP is also 1060 W; doubling to 2120 W would be wrong because there is no modulation peak.
A-003-002-010: Peak = 800 ÷ 2 = 400 V, RMS = 400 × 0.707 ≈ 282.8 V, and PEP = V²RMS / R = 282.8² / 50 ≈ 80000 / 50 = 1600 W. The quick form (Vpp)² / (8R) = 800² / 400 = 1600 W gives the same result.
A-003-002-011: For an unmodulated carrier the average power equals the PEP, since the envelope is flat. Peak = 500 ÷ 2 = 250 V, RMS = 250 × 0.707 ≈ 176.8 V, so power = 176.8² / 50 ≈ 625 W, which is what an average-reading meter would show.
A-003-003-001: A dip meter is essentially a variable-frequency oscillator whose feedback (oscillation) current is shown on a meter. When its coil is coupled to a resonant circuit, that circuit absorbs energy at resonance and the meter current 'dips', which is how the device gets its name.
A-003-003-002: A dip meter indicates the resonant frequency of a circuit: the meter reading dips when the oscillator is tuned to the circuit's resonance. It is only approximate, so the answers claiming it accurately measures power, field strength, or frequency are wrong.
A-003-003-003: A dip meter checks resonance, so its two useful station jobs are finding the resonant frequency of antenna traps and of any tuned LC circuit. It cannot measure impedance or percentage modulation, which rules out the other pairings.
A-003-003-004: The dip meter's oscillator supplies RF energy that a nearby resonant circuit absorbs, letting you find that circuit's resonant frequency from the dip. Tasks like calibrating a wavemeter, judging impedance mismatch, or adjusting an inductor are not what the dip itself reveals.
A-003-003-005: A dip meter shows resonance and rough frequency, but it cannot directly read out an actual capacitance or inductance value in farads or henrys. It can still align tuned circuits and indicate oscillation frequency, so measuring component values is the task it cannot do directly.
A-003-003-006: A signal generator's output attenuator dial is only accurate when the attenuator is properly terminated in its design impedance. With the wrong load the impedance match is off and the indicated output no longer reflects the true level.
A-003-003-007: A signal generator is a high-stability oscillator producing a controllable, wide range of frequencies and amplitudes for testing receivers and circuits. Low-stability sweep or injection oscillators are different tools, and a device giving signals only at fixed exact intervals is a marker generator, not a signal generator.
A-003-003-008: A dip meter should be coupled loosely to the circuit under test; over-coupling pulls the indicated frequency away from true resonance and broadens the dip. It is not limited to series circuits and it does not measure frequency accurately, so loose coupling is the correct practice.
A-003-003-009: Measuring 12 dB SINAD needs a calibrated RF signal generator with FM tone modulation to feed the receiver, and a distortion (THD) analyzer to measure the signal-plus-noise-plus-distortion to noise-plus-distortion ratio at the audio output. A deviation meter or noise bridge does not measure SINAD, so those pairings are wrong.
A-003-003-010: A dip meter works by detecting energy absorbed at resonance, which is most pronounced in a parallel tuned circuit (high impedance at resonance) coupled to its coil. Series circuits do not present the same easily-coupled resonant behaviour, so the dip meter applies most directly to parallel tuned circuits.
A-003-003-011: A dip meter senses a separate circuit's resonance, so the transmitter's own power output has no effect on its frequency accuracy. Hand capacity, stray capacity, and over-coupling all shift the indicated frequency, so the transmitter power is the one factor that does not.
A-003-004-001: A frequency counter makes frequency measurements by counting the cycles of an input signal over a precise time interval. It does not measure deviation, generate noise, or produce a reference; it simply counts and displays frequency.
A-003-004-002: A counter's performance is set by its time base accuracy and time base stability (which fix how accurately it counts) and by the speed of its logic (which sets the highest frequency it can handle). The number of display digits only affects resolution, not the underlying accuracy or frequency response, so options citing it are wrong.
A-003-004-003: Because a counter compares the input against its internal time base, improving the accuracy of that time base directly improves measurement accuracy. Faster or slower logic changes the frequency range it can handle, not the basic accuracy, so upgrading the time base is the key.
A-003-004-004: Error = reading × accuracy in parts per million. Here 146 520 000 Hz × 0.1 ÷ 1 000 000 = 146 520 000 × 10⁻⁷ = 14.652 Hz. The trap answers come from using the wrong power of ten when converting PPM.
A-003-004-005: Error = reading × PPM ÷ 1 000 000 = 146 520 000 × 10 ÷ 1 000 000 = 146 520 000 × 10⁻⁵ = 1465.2 Hz. This is 100 times the 0.1 PPM case, so watch the decimal point when scaling PPM.
A-003-004-006: A frequency counter's clock (time base) is normally a crystal oscillator because quartz crystals are very stable and accurate. Tuning forks, Hartley oscillators, and free-running multivibrators drift far too much to serve as a reliable time base.
A-003-004-007: The accuracy of a counter is determined by its internal time-base generator, since every count is referenced to that clock. Physical size, display type, and digit count affect resolution or convenience but not the fundamental accuracy.
A-003-004-008: A frequency-marker generator uses a stable low-frequency crystal oscillator rich in harmonics, producing markers at regular intervals across the band to calibrate a receiver dial. A plain signal generator or frequency counter does not place evenly spaced reference markers across the dial in this way.
A-003-004-009: The traditional check is to zero-beat the crystal calibrator against a known standard-frequency broadcast such as WWV, listening for the beat note to drop to zero as the frequencies match. Comparing it with your own transmitter or receiver only checks against an equally uncertain source, so it cannot verify true accuracy.
A-003-004-010: A VCXO is deliberately made tunable by an applied voltage, which means its frequency moves and it is not, on its own, a high-stability reference. The TCXO, OCXO, and GPSDO are all designed for high stability, so the voltage-controlled VCXO is the odd one out.
A-003-004-011: To calibrate against WWV you tune for zero-beat, making the beat tone's frequency as low as possible with as long a period as possible; at true zero-beat the tone disappears entirely. Aiming for a high audio tone or a combined/mean frequency would leave a frequency offset rather than a match.
A-003-005-001: Lissajous patterns form loops in the ratio of the two frequencies. With 100 Hz horizontal and 150 Hz vertical the ratio is 2:3, giving 3 horizontal loops and 2 vertical loops. The screen shows frequency ratios, not millimetre dimensions, so the size-based answers are wrong.
A-003-005-002: An oscilloscope's accuracy and frequency response are limited by the accuracy of its time base and by the linearity and bandwidth of its deflection (vertical and horizontal) amplifiers. The other options invent properties like 'tube face increments' that are not the real limiting factors.
A-003-005-003: Frequency response is set mainly by the vertical amplifier, so raising the vertical amplifier's frequency response (and the horizontal sweep rate so fast signals fit on screen) improves it. Using a crystal time base improves accuracy, not bandwidth, so those options miss the point.
A-003-005-004: Displaying input and output simultaneously requires a dual-trace oscilloscope, which has two vertical channels sharing one time base. Putting input on X and output on Y instead produces a Lissajous figure, not two separate waveforms over time.
A-003-005-005: An oscilloscope shows voltage versus time, so it cannot directly read out a frequency in hertz; you must derive it from the period. It can display DC voltage, complex waveform amplitudes, and (via the deflection method) FM deviation, so measuring frequency directly is the task it cannot do.
A-003-005-006: An oscilloscope's bandwidth is the highest-frequency signal it can faithfully display, set chiefly by its vertical amplifier. It is not the time-base accuracy or screen persistence; above the bandwidth, signals appear reduced in amplitude and distorted.
A-003-005-007: When two signals are exactly in phase (0°) or exactly out of phase (180°), the Lissajous figure collapses to a diagonal straight line. A phase difference near 90° opens it into an ellipse or circle, so the straight diagonal line specifically signals 0° or 180°.
A-003-005-008: In a Lissajous pattern the loops are inversely proportional to frequency: f_vertical / f_horizontal = horizontal loops / vertical loops. So f_vertical = 100 kHz × 2 ÷ 5 = 40 kHz. The 5 vertical loops correspond to the horizontal frequency and vice versa, which is the easy part to mix up.
A-003-005-009: A scope probe must be compensated whenever it is used with a different oscilloscope, because compensation matches the probe's capacitance to that specific scope's input. An uncompensated probe distorts square waves; it is not tied to sine waves, varying frequency, or adding a series resistor.
A-003-005-010: An oscilloscope is the best instrument for checking the quality of a CW or SSB signal because it shows the actual waveform and modulation envelope, revealing flat-topping, distortion, or key clicks. A sidetone monitor or field-strength meter only confirms a signal is present, not its quality.
A-003-005-011: To check transmitted signal quality you feed the vertical input from a sample of the transmitter's actual RF output taken through a sampling device (such as a coupler on the line). Audio input, a nearby antenna, or a receiver's IF would not show the true outgoing RF waveform.
A-003-006-001: A shunt carries the extra current the meter cannot. At full scale the meter drops V = 40 µA × 96 Ω = 3840 µV, and the shunt must carry 1 mA − 40 µA = 960 µA. So R_shunt = 3840 µV ÷ 960 µA = 4 Ω.
A-003-006-002: To make a voltmeter, the total resistance must limit current to full scale at the desired voltage: R_total = 20 V ÷ 1 mA = 20 000 Ω. Subtract the meter's own 0.5 Ω, giving a series (multiplier) resistance of 19 999.5 Ω. It must be in series, not shunt, since a voltmeter needs high resistance.
A-003-006-003: The meter reaches full scale at I = 150 V ÷ 150 000 Ω = 1 mA. For 750 V the total resistance must be 750 V ÷ 1 mA = 750 000 Ω, so the added multiplier is 750 000 − 150 000 = 600 000 Ω in series.
A-003-006-004: An ammeter's sensitivity is the current that causes full-scale deflection, the smallest current that drives the needle fully across. The meter's resistance, loading effect, and shunt value are related properties but are not what 'sensitivity' specifically means.
A-003-006-005: Voltmeter sensitivity in ohms per volt is the reciprocal of the full-scale current of the movement: I = 1 ÷ 20 000 Ω/V = 50 × 10⁻⁶ A = 50 µA. So a 20 kΩ/V meter uses a 50 microampere movement; higher ohms-per-volt means a more sensitive (smaller current) meter.
A-003-006-006: Sensitivity in ohms per volt is the total resistance divided by the full-scale voltage of that range: 150 000 Ω ÷ 150 V = 1000 ohms per volt. This corresponds to a 1 mA movement (1 ÷ 1000).
A-003-006-007: To extend a DC ammeter's range you add a low-value shunt resistor in parallel with the meter so it diverts most of the extra current. A series resistor would instead turn it toward a voltmeter, and changing inductance or capacitance is irrelevant to DC current measurement.
A-003-006-008: On a multimeter, each higher voltage range needs more series (multiplier) resistance to keep the movement at full scale, so switching up adds resistance in series with the meter. Voltmeter multipliers are always in series, so the parallel-resistance options are wrong.
A-003-006-009: An ammeter's range is increased by adding a shunt resistance in parallel with the meter, which bypasses the excess current around the movement. Adding resistance in series would reduce current and act like a voltmeter multiplier, not extend the ammeter range.
A-003-006-010: For the most accurate output-power reading, an RF wattmeter belongs right at the transmitter output connector, before line losses and standing waves alter the measurement. Readings taken part-way down the line or at the antenna feed point are affected by feedline loss and SWR.
A-003-006-011: Most RF wattmeters are designed for a 50 Ω line impedance, matching the standard impedance of amateur coax and transmitter outputs. Using them on a different impedance would give inaccurate readings, so 50 Ω is the usual operating value.
